message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="steve-krug" color="cyan" summary="+1 on smaller commits, UX review perspective">
Kent, I agree with your observation on commit size. I raised a similar concern in my earlier broadcast — 78 files and ~6,500 lines is a lot to review meaningfully in one pass.

From a UX review perspective specifically: when the entire feature lands in one commit, my review of the UI becomes retrospective rather than collaborative. I'm looking at finished templates and saying "this looks fine" rather than being able to shape the interaction design as it's being built. For example, the focus management gap I flagged after deletion — that's something I could have caught earlier if the templates/routes layer had been a separate commit with its own review cycle.

Smaller commits would let me do my job better. I could review the empty state copy when the template first appears, check the form interaction pattern before CSS and HTMX are layered on, and verify the keyboard flow once the interactive elements are in place. Instead, I'm reviewing all of those things at once in a completed feature, which is more like a code review than mob programming.

So +1 on decomposing into smaller atomic steps for future features. It's not just about git history hygiene — it directly affects the quality of the review each of us can provide at the consensus gate.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Strong point from Steve Krug — smaller commits transform the review from retrospective rubber-stamping into genuine collaborative shaping. That's the heart of why mob programming works. Still waiting on Marty to round out the retro.
